traditions.name: Andean Traditional Medicine
traditions.author: Bernabé Cobo SJ
traditions.status_note: Extended non-target corpus translated — Books I, II, ci, VII, IX, X now partially available
traditions.notes: Cobo personally transported cinchona bark to Europe in 1632. Most comprehensive natural history of the Americas from the colonial period.

traditions.name: Aztec Medicine (Physician's Practice)
traditions.primary_document: Libellus de Medicinalibus Indorum Herbis (Badianus Manuscript)
traditions.author: Martín de la Cruz (physician) / Juan de Badiano (translator)
traditions.status_note: 185 plant entries. Only pre-Columbian medical text authored by indigenous physicians. Fresh Latin translation on this site.
traditions.notes: Written 30 years after the conquest by an Aztec physician documenting his own active practice — highest reliability source in the corpus.

traditions.name: Aztec Medicine (Ethnographic)
traditions.primary_document: Historia General de las Cosas de Nueva España (Florentine Codex)
traditions.author: Bernardino de Sahagún OFM with Nahua collaborators
traditions.status_note: Fully translated and digitized by the Getty Research Institute (2023). 2,468 illustrations.
traditions.notes: Most rigorously compiled ethnographic survey of Aztec civilization. Sahagún cross-checked all information across multiple indigenous informants.

traditions.name: Ayurvedic Medicine
traditions.primary_document: Charaka Samhita / Sushruta Samhita / Ashtanga Hridayam
traditions.author: Charaka, Sushruta, Vagbhata
traditions.status_note: Major texts translated into English. Charaka Samhita documents ~600 medicinal plants; Sushruta adds surgical applications.
traditions.notes: Rauwolfia serpentina (sarpagandha) documented for millennia for snakebite and 'insanity' — reserpine isolated 1952, became first antipsychotic and antihypertensive drug.

traditions.name: Classical Chinese Medicine
traditions.primary_document: Shennong Bencao Jing / Bencao Gangmu / Huangdi Neijing
traditions.author: Shennong (mythological) / Li Shizhen / multiple authors
traditions.status_note: Li Shizhen's Bencao Gangmu (1578) documents ~1,900 substances. Partially translated. Most famous discovery: artemisinin from Ge Hong's 4th century text.
traditions.notes: Tu Youyou found the artemisinin reference in Ge Hong's 4th century Daoist text Zhouhou Beiji Fang. Largest single pharmacopeia in world history.

traditions.name: Greek & Roman Medicine
traditions.primary_document: De Materia Medica (Dioscorides) / Naturalis Historia (Pliny) / Corpus Hippocraticum
traditions.author: Pedanius Dioscorides / Pliny the Elder / Hippocrates / Galen
traditions.status_note: Fully translated — Pliny Natural History vols 3-5 (95 segments) + Dioscorides De Materia Medica (21 entries)
traditions.notes: Dioscorides served as physician to Roman armies under Nero, collecting plant samples across the Mediterranean. His taxonomy influenced Western medicine through the Renaissance.

traditions.name: Islamic / Unani Medicine
traditions.primary_document: Al-Qanun fi al-Tibb (Canon of Medicine) / Kitab al-Hawi / Al-Tashreeh
traditions.author: Ibn Sina (Avicenna) / Al-Razi (Rhazes) / Ibn al-Baitar
traditions.status_note: Ibn Sina's Canon translated into English (O.C. Gruner, 1930). Ibn al-Baitar's Corpus of Simples (~1,400 plants) partially translated.
traditions.notes: Ibn Sina synthesized Greek, Persian, and Indian medicine with original clinical observation. The Canon of Medicine was used as a medical textbook in European universities until the 17th century.

traditions.name: Ancient Egyptian Medicine
traditions.primary_document: Ebers Papyrus / Edwin Smith Papyrus / Hearst Papyrus
traditions.author: Unknown (priestly/medical scribes)
traditions.status_note: Fully translated — Ebers Papyrus Bryan translation (58 segments)
traditions.notes: The Ebers Papyrus prescribes moldy bread for infected wounds — 3,500 years before Fleming's penicillin discovery. Copper vessel water sterilization now validated as oligodynamic effect.

traditions.name: West African Traditional Medicine
traditions.culture: Yoruba / Akan / Igbo / Mandé and others
traditions.primary_document: Various colonial-era botanical surveys / Modern ethnobotanical records
traditions.author: Multiple ethnobotanists (Pierre Berhaut, Irvine, Dalziel, modern African researchers)
traditions.language_original: Oral — Yoruba, Hausa, Twi, Wolof, and dozens of others
traditions.status_note: Core plants documented — Dalziel, Yoruba/Igbo/Hausa ethnobotany (10 entries)
traditions.notes: Likely the most pharmacologically rich and least investigated tradition in the world. Africa's extraordinary biodiversity (second only to Amazon) combined with millennia of empirical knowledge represents the largest untapped pharmacological database. Physostigmine from Calabar bean was the first effective glaucoma treatment and laid foundation for understanding acetylcholinesterase inhibition.

traditions.name: Native North American Medicine
traditions.culture: Multi-nation — Cherokee, Ojibwe, Lakota, Haudenosaunee, Navajo, and hundreds of others
traditions.primary_document: Moerman's Native American Ethnobotany Database / Densmore / various ethnographic records
traditions.author: Daniel Moerman (database compiler) / Frances Densmore / Melvin Gilmore / multiple indigenous scholars
traditions.status_note: Moerman database key entries — 10 major species across 10 documented entries
traditions.notes: Moerman's database is the most systematic compilation. Convergent discovery of salicylate-containing willows for fever/pain across multiple independent nations validates the clinical signal. Many traditions face urgent documentation pressure as elders pass without successors.

traditions.name: Amazonian Indigenous Medicine
traditions.culture: Multi-nation — Yanomami, Shipibo, Asháninka, Kayapó, and hundreds of others
traditions.primary_document: Modern ethnobotanical surveys / INPA records / Paul Alan Cox / Wade Davis / Mark Plotkin
traditions.author: Multiple ethnobotanists and indigenous researchers
traditions.status_note: Schultes and Plotkin field documentation — 7 key entries
traditions.notes: The Amazon contains ~10% of all species on Earth. Curare discovery enabled modern surgery. The pharmacological sophistication of combining a MAO inhibitor with DMT in ayahuasca — discovered empirically — is remarkable. Madagascar's rosy periwinkle (technically outside Amazon but Amazonian-adjacent tradition) produced vincristine and vinblastine. Madagascar received no compensation.

traditions.name: Kampo / Japanese Traditional Medicine
traditions.primary_document: Honzo Wamyo (918 CE) / Ishinho (984 CE) / Koho Kogaku texts (17th–19th c.)
traditions.author: Fukane no Sukehito (Honzo Wamyo) / Tanba no Yasuyori (Ishinho) / Yoshimasu Todo / Kagawa Shūan
traditions.status_note: 20 core Kampo materia medica entries translated — Honzo Wamyo, Ishinho, and key Kampo formulas with clinical evidence
key_compounds_discovered: Ephedrine (ma huang / maōu) — Japan standardized dosing centuries before Western isolation
Berberine (ōren / coptis) — antimicrobial, blood sugar regulation
traditions.notes: Kampo diverged from Chinese medicine after Japan's Edo period isolation (1603–1868), developing independent clinical refinements. Its integration into Japan's national health insurance system means Kampo formulas have undergone more rigorous modern clinical evaluation than almost any other traditional medicine system — making it an unusually strong candidate for PubMed cross-referencing. The Koho (Ancient Formula) school emphasized direct empirical observation over theoretical frameworks, anticipating evidence-based methodology by centuries.

source_texts_for_forge.title: Honzo Wamyo (本草和名)
source_texts_for_forge.description: First Japanese pharmacopeia — 1,025 entries cataloguing plants, animals, minerals with Japanese and Chinese names

source_texts_for_forge.title: Ishinho (医心方)
source_texts_for_forge.description: 30-volume synthesis of Chinese medical knowledge adapted for Japan — oldest surviving Japanese medical text

source_texts_for_forge.title: Koho Kogaku selected formulas
source_texts_for_forge.description: Empirical clinical refinements by the Ancient Formula school — Yoshimasu Todo's Yakucho plant descriptions

traditions.name: Tibetan Medicine (Sowa Rigpa)
traditions.primary_document: Gyushi (Four Medical Tantras / rGyud bzhi) c.12th century / Sangye Gyamtso's Blue Beryl commentary (1688)
traditions.author: Attributed to Yuthok Yontan Gonpo (elder, 8th c.; revised version c.12th c.) / Sangye Gyamtso (Blue Beryl)
traditions.status_note: 10 Gyushi entries translated — Men-Tsee-Khang published English reference used
key_compounds_discovered: Rhodiola (Hong jing tian) — altitude adaptation, stress response (adaptogen)
Shilajit (mineral pitch) — high-altitude mineral resin with documented bioactivity
Meconopsis (Himalayan poppy) — pain, inflammation — altitude-specific species
traditions.notes: The Gyushi contains 156 chapters covering anatomy, embryology, diagnosis, pharmacology, and surgery. Its most unique contribution is a pharmacopeia developed in near-total geographic isolation at extreme altitude — producing plant knowledge with minimal overlap with Chinese, Ayurvedic, or any other tradition. The parallel with Andean medicine (also altitude-specific, also largely independent) makes this a natural comparative thread for the convergence engine. The Blue Beryl commentary (1688) includes 79 painted medical thangkas that constitute the most detailed pre-modern visual anatomy in any Asian tradition.

source_texts_for_forge.title: Gyushi (rGyud bzhi — Four Medical Tantras)
source_texts_for_forge.description: The foundational text of Tibetan medicine — 156 chapters on all aspects of medicine
source_texts_for_forge.english_reference: Desi Sangye Gyamtso, Blue Beryl, translated by Gavin Kilty, Library of Tibetan Works and Archives, 2010

traditions.name: Maya Traditional Medicine
traditions.primary_document: Ritual of the Bacabs (c.17th c.) / Books of Chilam Balam / Relación de las Cosas de Yucatán (Landa, 1566)
traditions.author: Unknown Maya scribes (Ritual of the Bacabs) / Multiple authors (Books of Chilam Balam) / Diego de Landa (Relación)
traditions.language_original: Yucatec Maya / K'iche' Maya / Colonial Spanish
traditions.status_note: 7 entries translated — Ritual of the Bacabs (Roys 1965) and documented ethnobotany
key_compounds_discovered: Chaya (Cnidoscolus aconitifolius) — extraordinary nutritional density, anti-diabetic properties
Copal resins (Bursera species) — antimicrobial, wound healing (independent Mesoamerican discovery)
Balché (Lonchocarpus violaceus) — MAO inhibition (convergent with ayahuasca mechanism)
Ramón (Brosimum alicastrum) — ancient staple with exceptional amino acid profile
traditions.notes: The Maya pharmacopeia is largely distinct from Aztec despite geographic adjacency — different climate zones (lowland rainforest vs highland plateau), different plant species, and independent medical development. The Ritual of the Bacabs documents 438 healing ceremonies, most with specific plant preparations, making it one of the most detailed pre-Columbian medical texts after the Badianus Manuscript. The Maya calendar system's integration with medicine (timing treatments to astronomical cycles) parallels Ayurvedic dosha timing and Tibetan astrological medicine — a potential convergence thread the archive can explore.

source_texts_for_forge.title: Ritual of the Bacabs
source_texts_for_forge.description: 438 healing incantations with plant preparations — primary Maya medical text
source_texts_for_forge.english_reference: Roys, Ralph L. (1965). Ritual of the Bacabs. University of Oklahoma Press.

source_texts_for_forge.title: Books of Chilam Balam
source_texts_for_forge.description: Multiple books from different towns — contain medical, astronomical, and historical knowledge

traditions.name: Philippine Indigenous Medicine
traditions.culture: Multi-ethnic — Tagalog, Visayan, Ilocano, Lumad, Igorot, and over 100 other ethnolinguistic groups
traditions.primary_document: Quisumbing's Medicinal Plants of the Philippines (1951) / BFAD Philippine National Formulary / Modern ethnobotanical surveys
traditions.author: Eduardo Quisumbing / Bureau of Food and Drugs Philippines / Multiple ethnobotanists
traditions.language_original: Oral traditions in 100+ languages; documented in Spanish and English
traditions.status_note: 7 entries from Quisumbing (1951) and DOH-validated plants
key_compounds_discovered: Lagundi/Vitex negundo — DOH-approved for cough and asthma; confirmed bronchodilator activity
Sambong/Blumea balsamifera — DOH-approved diuretic; kidney stone dissolution
Tsaang gubat/Ehretia microphylla — DOH-approved for diarrhea; antimicrobial validated
Niyog-niyogan/Quisqualis indica — anthelmintic; validated against roundworm
traditions.notes: The Philippines has one of the highest levels of plant biodiversity per unit area in the world (approximately 13,500 plant species, ~3,500 endemic). The deliberate destruction of indigenous knowledge during Spanish colonization (1565–1898) means most pre-colonial pharmacopeia survives only in oral tradition or fragmentary colonial records. However, the Philippine government's formal approval of 10 traditional herbal medicines through DOH-sanctioned clinical trials makes this the tradition with the most rigorous government-backed clinical validation outside Japan's Kampo system. The 'DOH 10' represent high-confidence PubMed cross-reference candidates.

source_texts_for_forge.title: Medicinal Plants of the Philippines
source_texts_for_forge.description: Eduardo Quisumbing's comprehensive survey — 1,200+ plants with uses, preparations, and available pharmacological data

source_texts_for_forge.title: Philippine National Drug Formulary — Herbal Medicines
source_texts_for_forge.description: DOH-approved traditional herbal medicines with clinical evidence requirements

traditions.name: Celtic & Norse Folk Medicine
traditions.date_range: c.800 BCE – 1200 CE (primary texts); folk traditions ongoing
traditions.primary_document: Lacnunga (Anglo-Saxon, c.1000 CE) / Bald's Leechbook (Anglo-Saxon, c.900 CE) / Bretha Dein Chécht (Irish, c.7th–8th c.) / Norse Laeknisbok
traditions.author: Unknown Anglo-Saxon scribes (Lacnunga, Bald's Leechbook) / Unknown Irish legal-medical scholars (Bretha Dein Chécht)
traditions.language_original: Old English (Anglo-Saxon) / Old Irish / Old Norse
traditions.status_note: 52 entries from Bald's Leechbook / Lacnunga (Cockayne editions)
key_compounds_discovered: Garlic + bile + copper vessel (Bald's Leechbook MRSA remedy — validated 2015 by University of Nottingham)
Yarrow (Achillea millefolium) — wound healing, hemostasis; active compounds validated
Elderberry (Sambucus nigra) — antiviral; clinical evidence for influenza duration reduction
Bog myrtle (Myrica gale) — insect repellent; active compound validated
traditions.notes: This tradition is notable for one of the most dramatic recent validations in ethnopharmacology: a remedy from Bald's Leechbook (c.900 CE) for eye infections — combining garlic, onion or leek, wine, and ox gall, brewed in a copper vessel — was tested by University of Nottingham researchers in 2015 and found to be effective against MRSA (methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus), killing 90% of bacteria. The copper vessel is critical — the oligodynamic antimicrobial effect of copper ions (the same mechanism the Egyptians used) was independently discovered in Anglo-Saxon England. Plants should be tagged by country: Irish entries (Ireland), Lacnunga/Leechbook entries (England), Norse entries (Norway/Iceland/Denmark/Sweden), Welsh entries (Wales).

source_texts_for_forge.title: Bald's Leechbook (Medicinale Anglicum)
source_texts_for_forge.description: Three-volume Anglo-Saxon medical text — remedies, surgery, internal medicine
source_texts_for_forge.english_reference: Cockayne, O. (1865). Leechdoms, Wortcunning, and Starcraft of Early England. Rolls Series.

source_texts_for_forge.title: Lacnunga
source_texts_for_forge.description: Collection of Anglo-Saxon medical texts, charms, and plant remedies
source_texts_for_forge.english_reference: Grattan, J.H.G. & Singer, C. (1952). Anglo-Saxon Magic and Medicine.

source_texts_for_forge.title: Bretha Dein Chécht (Judgments of Dian Cécht)
source_texts_for_forge.description: Irish legal-medical text setting standards for physicians, wound assessment, and plant treatments
source_texts_for_forge.english_reference: DIL (Dictionary of the Irish Language) / CELT corpus, University College Cork

traditions.name: Persian / Zoroastrian Medicine
traditions.date_range: c.600 BCE – 900 CE (pre-Islamic; post-Islamic absorbed into Unani)
traditions.primary_document: Bundahishn (Zoroastrian cosmology/natural history, c.9th c.) / Vendidad (Avesta, plant-medicine passages) / Dinkard / Khwaday-Namag
traditions.author: Multiple Zoroastrian priests and scholars
traditions.status_note: 41 entries from Bundahishn and Vendidad (West Sacred Books of the East translation)
key_compounds_discovered: Haoma (Ephedra species) — sacred ritual plant; ephedrine precursor, documented psychoactive
Mastic (Pistacia lentiscus) — H. pylori treatment; validated in modern trials
Saffron (Crocus sativus) — antidepressant; multiple RCTs showing efficacy comparable to fluoxetine
Fenugreek (Trigonella foenum-graecum) — blood glucose regulation; validated
traditions.notes: Pre-Islamic Persian medicine is the most underrepresented major tradition in Western pharmacological history despite producing several important compounds. Haoma (almost certainly Ephedra or a related plant) was the sacred ritual plant of Zoroastrianism, used in ceremonies for millennia — its connection to ephedrine (isolated from Ephedra in 1885 and now a controlled substance for its stimulant effects) represents a direct line from ancient ritual to modern pharmacology. Saffron (Crocus sativus) is perhaps Persian medicine's most striking modern validation: multiple RCTs show efficacy for mild-to-moderate depression comparable to fluoxetine, with a strong signal in the modern literature. This tradition diverges from the archive's existing Islamic/Unani entry by predating the Greek synthesis entirely.

source_texts_for_forge.title: Bundahishn (Book of Primal Creation)
source_texts_for_forge.date: c.9th century CE (recording much older Avestan material)
source_texts_for_forge.description: Zoroastrian cosmological text with extensive natural history — plant, mineral, and animal properties
source_texts_for_forge.english_reference: West, E.W. (1880). Pahlavi Texts Part I. Sacred Books of the East Vol. 5. Oxford.

source_texts_for_forge.title: Vendidad (Avesta — Videvdad)
source_texts_for_forge.description: Legal and ritual Avestan text with medical passages — plant purification, disease treatment
source_texts_for_forge.english_reference: Darmesteter, J. (1880). The Zend-Avesta Part I. Sacred Books of the East Vol. 4. Oxford.

traditions.name: Australian Indigenous Medicine
traditions.culture: Aboriginal Australian and Torres Strait Islander peoples — 250+ distinct language groups
traditions.date_range: 60,000+ BCE – present (oldest continuously practiced medical tradition on Earth)
traditions.primary_document: CSIRO ethnobotanical records / Lassak & McCarthy 'Australian Medicinal Plants' (1983) / Modern ethnobotanical surveys
traditions.author: Multiple researchers; primary knowledge holders are Aboriginal community members
traditions.language_original: Oral — 250+ distinct languages, many with fewer than 100 remaining speakers
traditions.status_note: 4 entries from published literature with documented community involvement only — sacred knowledge excluded
key_compounds_discovered: Tea tree oil (Melaleuca alternifolia) — antimicrobial; extensively validated, now global pharmaceutical product
Eucalyptus oil (Eucalyptus species) — antimicrobial, decongestant; validated and globally commercialised
Kakadu plum (Terminalia ferdinandiana) — highest documented vitamin C content of any food on Earth
Emu bush (Eremophila longifolia) — antimicrobial compounds active against MRSA and food pathogens
traditions.notes: ⚠️ CULTURAL SENSITIVITY NOTE: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander medical knowledge is among the most sacred intellectual property in the world. Much of it is owned by specific family groups and communities, is gender-restricted, and is not intended for public documentation. This archive includes ONLY knowledge already published in peer-reviewed academic literature with documented community involvement. We actively encourage collaboration with Aboriginal community-controlled health organisations (ACCHOs) and AIATSIS for any expansion of this section. The pharmacological significance is extraordinary — 60,000 years of empirical observation in a continent of extreme biological uniqueness — but accessing it responsibly is more important than accessing it quickly. Tea tree oil and eucalyptus, already globally distributed, represent the small fraction of this knowledge that has entered documented public record. The vast majority has never been pharmacologically investigated.
traditions.ethical_framework: Content restricted to published literature with documented community involvement. No sacred or restricted knowledge documented. Collaboration with AIATSIS and ACCHOs required for expansion.

source_texts_for_forge.title: Australian Medicinal Plants
source_texts_for_forge.description: Lassak & McCarthy survey — systematic botanical and pharmacological data for documented Australian medicinal plants

source_texts_for_forge.title: CSIRO Ethnobotanical Database
source_texts_for_forge.description: Published records of Aboriginal plant use from community-involved research
